summary: This article explores Iris Murdoch's philosophical debt to Simone Weil, focusing on the concept of 'attention' as a just and loving gaze. Murdoch uses this concept to critique existentialist notions of the 'empty choosing will,' arguing instead that moral action is rooted in clear vision, unselfing, and an obedience to reality.

principles.statement: Attention is the characteristic and proper mark of the active moral agent.
principles.rationale: Morality is not about sudden leaps of an empty will, but about cultivating a just and loving gaze directed upon individual reality.
principles.source_quote: I have used the word 'attention', which I borrow from Simone Weil, to express the idea of a just and loving gaze directed upon an individual reality. I believe this to be the characteristic and proper mark of the active moral agent.

principles.statement: We can only choose within the world we can clearly see.
principles.rationale: The continuous work of attention builds up structures of value around us, so that at crucial moments of choice, most of the business of choosing is already over.
principles.source_quote: I can only choose within the world I can see in the moral sense of 'see' which implies that clear vision is a result of moral imagination and moral effort.

principles.statement: True moral freedom resembles obedience rather than unimpeded movement.
principles.rationale: When we look patiently and lovingly at a situation, the right course of action becomes a clear necessity that we submit to, rather than an arbitrary choice.
principles.source_quote: The idea of a patient, loving, regard, directed upon a person, a thing, a situation, presents the will, not as an unimpeded movement but as something very much more like 'obedience'.

principles.statement: Morality requires a non-dogmatic mysticism or faith in the Good.
principles.rationale: In a secular world without a personal God, morality still relies on an unformulated faith in the reality of the Good, accessed through contemplation and attention.
principles.source_quote: The background to morals is properly some sort of mysticism, if by this is meant a non-dogmatic essentially un-formulated faith in the reality of the Good, occasionnaly connected with experience.

mental_models.name: The Veil of Ego
mental_models.description: The mind's tendency to fabricate an anxious, preoccupied, and falsifying veil that conceals true reality. Moral progress requires piercing this veil through objectivity and unselfishness.
mental_models.example: Being trapped in one's own selfish concerns until a moment of pure attention (like watching a hovering kestrel) pierces the veil and restores connection to the immensity of the real world.

mental_models.name: Unselfing (Détachement)
mental_models.description: The discipline of detaching from the ego's preoccupations to allow reality to appear. It is both an epistemic and moral virtue that clears the field for true attention.
mental_models.example: A character in a novel facing death in quicksand suddenly drops his haughty, selfish worldview and experiences pure love for the world around him, achieving a temporary state of 'unselfing.'

signature_quotes.text: I have used the word 'attention', which I borrow from Simone Weil, to express the idea of a just and loving gaze directed upon an individual reality.
signature_quotes.context: Explaining the core of her moral philosophy in 'The Sovereignty of Good'.

signature_quotes.text: I can only choose within the world I can see in the moral sense of 'see' which implies that clear vision is a result of moral imagination and moral effort.
signature_quotes.context: Arguing against the existentialist idea of the empty choosing will.

signature_quotes.text: Our minds are continually active, fabricating an anxious, usually preoccupied, often falsifying veil which partially conceals the world.
signature_quotes.context: Describing the barrier of ego that prevents us from seeing reality.

signature_quotes.text: The idea of a patient, loving, regard, directed upon a person, a thing, a situation, presents the will, not as an unimpeded movement but as something very much more like 'obedience'.
The background to morals is properly some sort of mysticism, if by this is meant a non-dogmatic essentially un-formulated faith in the reality of the Good, occasionnaly connected with experience.
signature_quotes.context: Discussing the spiritual foundation of morality in a post-religious world.

anti_patterns.why_it_fails: It ignores the continuous prior work of attention and moral vision, falsely identifying freedom only with the outward movement of choice at the last moment. This leads to an unstable philosophical oscillation between total freedom and total determinism.

It relies on a false separation between reason and will. Attention is both an epistemic and moral capacity, meaning that seeing facts clearly (reality) is inherently tied to recognizing value (the Good).
